data.title: UBS already has enough capital to meet proposed new rules - Swiss central bank
data.description:
UBS Group (UBS) already has sufficient capital to meet the proposed new rules, according to the Swiss central bank.

A proposed legislation in Switzerland requires UBS to fully back its foreign units with ​common equity Tier 1 capital. The new rule is intended to toughen legislation to prevent a repeat of the Credit Suisse meltdown.

The new rule was anticipated to require UBS to strengthen its CET1 capital by ~$20B. The Swiss bank was opposing the proposal, saying that they would hurt its ability to compete globally.

Notably, the Financial Stability Report 2026 released by the Swiss National Bank today said, "UBS exceeds its fully applied capital requirements under the current 'too big to fail' ((TBTF)) regulations applicable as of 2030."

"Regarding liquidity, experience from 2022 – 2023 in Switzerland and the U.S. shows that outflows can deplete liquidity buffers rapidly, even if these buffers are substantial. Some banks may also face the risk of liquidity shortfalls in foreign currencies. It is therefore essential that banks prepare sufficient collateral eligible for accessing liquidity support from the SNB (Extended Liquidity Facility, ELF) and, where applicable, from foreign central banks," said the report.

"The proposal of the Federal Council addresses these risks in a targeted manner and primarily affects UBS," said the report.

"According to the pro forma calculations of the authorities and including reserves, UBS already has sufficient capital to meet the proposed requirements," said the report.

data.title: U.S. stocks get a lift from weaker-than-expected payrolls lowering odds of a rate hike
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes inched higher on Thursday due to a weaker-than-expected June non-farm payrolls report that lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.6%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was +0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.9%, adding to new highs.

The U.S. economy added 57,000 jobs in June, falling short of the 115,000 increase economists surveyed by Dow Jones had expected. The unemployment rate, however, unexpectedly declined to 4.2%, versus forecasts for it to hold at 4.3%.

Moreover, initial jobless claims fell to 215,000 for the week ended June 27, beating estimates.

Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh said on Wednesday that the Fed will “chart a new course” on forward guidance, contrasting with his predecessor's practice of indicating which way policymakers were leaning in upcoming meetings.

Odds of an interest rate hike before the end of the year fell, while odds for a 25-basis point cut rose, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Bond yields were slightly lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower at 4.48%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 3 basis points to 4.15%.

In addition, the U.S. and Iran are conducting negotiations in Doha that focus on the Strait of Hormuz. The Trump administration is trying to convince Iran that it will gain more from a nuclear deal than from tolls in the strait.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $67, while Brent (CO1:COM) was $70 per barrel.

data.title: MP Materials, Cleveland-Cliffs lead short interest among materials stocks above $2B
data.description:
Among materials stocks with market capitalizations above $2B, MP Materials (MP) carries the highest short interest at 16.21% of float, followed by Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) at 13.51%, highlighting sustained bearish positioning in select metals, mining, and chemicals companies despite broader strength across parts of the materials sector.

In contrast, the least shorted names among materials stocks with market capitalizations above $2B are Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM), with short interest of just 0.65% of float, followed by AngloGold Ashanti (AU) at 0.82%, reflecting relatively limited bearish sentiment toward these companies.

Overall, short interest remains concentrated in a handful of materials stocks, while many larger, more established names continue to attract relatively limited bearish positioning.

Here are the five most shorted materials stocks with market capitalizations above $2B (as a percentage of shares outstanding):

  • MP Materials Corp. (MP), Short interest: 16.21%
  • Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (CLF), Short interest: 13.51%
  • Olin Corporation (OLN), Short interest: 13.34%
  • Alpha Metallurgical Resources, Inc. (AMR), Short interest: 13.20%
  • Ashland Inc. (ASH), Short interest: 12.27%

Here are the five least shorted materials stocks with market capitalizations above $2B (as a percentage of shares outstanding):

  • Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A. (SQM), Short interest: 0.65%
  • AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU), Short interest: 0.82%
  • Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM), Short interest: 0.88%
  • Greif, Inc. (GEF.B), Short interest: 1.13%
  • Ecolab Inc. (ECL), Short interest: 1.30%

data.title: AI memory, chip stocks mixed after Wall Street opens higher
data.description:
Shares of memory and AI-related stocks were mixed on Thursday, trying to rebound after yesterday's sell-off.

Wall Street averages opened higher after June’s weaker-than-expected nonfarm payrolls report tempered expectations for further Federal Reserve rate hikes.  The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose about 0.72%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) climbed about 0.73%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow (DJI) rose about 0.85%.

Wednesday's tech sell-off had pressured Asian peers, which opened in the red and tumbled during the day. Several AI and chip stocks were also down premarket in the U.S. before opening in the green. South Korea’s Kospi Index slumped about 7.89%. South Korean memory chip giant SK Hynix (HXSCL) plummeted nearly 15%, while peer Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) tumbled around 9% on the Korea Exchange. Earlier this week, the two countries pledged billions in investment in South Korea aimed at growing the country's semiconductors and AI space. Today the two provided details. 

SK Hynix said it would invest 100T won (about $64.38B) to build memory chip ‌and packaging plants as part of a South Korean investment drive, which was announced earlier this week, aimed at semiconductors and AI. Meanwhile, Samsung plans to invest a combined 140T won ($90B) in South Korea's central Chungcheong region, expanding production of display panels, batteries, semiconductors, and chip materials. Reportedly, SK Hynix is removing price caps from its Long-Term Agreements, or LTAs, recently signed with its clients. Analysts suggest that suppliers' negotiating power is being significantly strengthened as the company, along with rival Samsung, extends contract periods.

"Samsung and SK Hynix now make up around half the Kospi’s total weight, up from around just a quarter at the end of last year," Zavier Wong, analyst at eToro, said in a note, according to CNBC. This highlights the companies' huge influence on the country’s benchmark index.

Hong Kong and Chinese tech stocks also saw red. China’s largest contract chipmaker and foundry Semiconductor Manufacturing International tumbled about 10% in Hong Kong, while Hua Hong Grace, another Chinese chip foundry listed in Hong Kong, slumped nearly 13%.

American memory giant Micron Technology (MU) — which competes with Samsung and SK Hynix to provide High-Bandwidth Memory, or HBM chips to Nvidia — rose nearly 2% on Thursday. Western Digital (WDC) and Seagate Technology (STX) each climbed around 1%, while Sandisk (SNDK) fell nearly 1%.

Shares of AI chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) were largely flat but in the green. Qualcomm (QCOM) and Broadcom (AVGO) were also among the gains, but Cerebras (CBRS) dipped about 1%.

Other AI and networking-related stocks were mixed on Thursday. Arista Networks (ANET) rose about 1%, while Corning (GLW) also saw some gains. However, Ciena (CIEN) fell about 2%, while Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) and Cisco (CSCO) each dipped nearly 1%. Coherent (COHR), Celestica (CLS), and Lumentum (LITE) were largely flat but in the red.

Shares of Arm (ARM) jumped about 6%, while Intel (INTC) climbed nearly 3% after Wedbush noted that Tesla's hiring of Gary Jiang, an 18-year veteran of Intel, to help with its Terafab project is considered a potential “positive” for the chip giant. Meanwhile, HSBC raised Intel's price target to a street-high of $200 from $100, while keeping its Buy rating.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) rose around 3%, while Texas Instruments (TXN) rose about 2%. Analog Devices (ADI) climbed nearly 1%, while GlobalFoundries (GFS) was also in the green. However, Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) fell about 2%, while Marvell Technology (MRVL) was also in the red.

Chip equipment makers:  KLA (KLAC) fell about 4%,  while Lam Research (LRCX) and Applied Materials (AMAT) each declined nearly 3%. However, ASML (ASML) was largely flat but in the green on Thursday.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement and additional information.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: CMS rule aims to lower Medicare costs at hospitals, ambulatory service centers
data.description:
• A proposed rule from CMS would change the way hospital outpatient departments and ambulatory surgical centers are paid for services and drugs provided to Medicare enrollees starting in 2027.
  • One of the key payment changes is for medications purchased under the 340B Drug Pricing Program. The agency noted that a survey it conducted found that many hospitals acquire 340B drugs at an average cost significantly below the Average Sales Price. 
  • "By aligning payments with documented acquisition costs, CMS estimates this proposal would save people with Original Medicare a collective estimated $1.15 billion in drug costs and save taxpayers an additional estimated $4.55 billion in drug expenditures, for total reduced drugs spending of approximately $5.7 billion in 2027 alone," CMS stated.
  • Another element would end the payment discrepancies in imaging services, such as X-rays and MRIs, when they are conducted in a hospital rather than an outpatient clinic. CMS said this would cut expenditures by $260M in the first year — ~$190M in Part B savings and ~$70M in reduced beneficiary premiums.

data.title: 10 most shorted U.S. tech stocks above $2B market cap
data.description:
Investor skepticism remains elevated across several technology and digital infrastructure companies, with Wolfspeed (WOLF) leading the list of the most shorted U.S. stocks in the sector with market capitalizations above $2B.

The semiconductor company had a short interest of 53.77%, according to the latest data.

The list spans a broad range of industries, including semiconductors, AI software, communications equipment, digital infrastructure, quantum computing, and bitcoin mining, highlighting where bearish bets remain concentrated despite continued investor enthusiasm for emerging technologies.

Here are the 10 most shorted technology stocks with market capitalizations above $2B:

  • Wolfspeed (WOLF) – Semiconductors: 53.77% short interest
  • SoundHound AI (SOUN) – Application Software: 40.21%
  • CleanSpark (CLSK) – Application Software: 33.04%
  • Ondas Holdings (ONDS) – Communications Equipment: 33.00%
  • UiPath (PATH) – Systems Software: 27.84%
  • MARA Holdings (MARA) – Application Software: 26.90%
  • Applied Digital (APLD) – Internet Services & Infrastructure: 26.02%
  • Quantum Computing (QUBT) – Technology Hardware, Storage & Peripherals: 26.01%
  • Core Scientific (CORZ) – Application Software: 23.51%
  • TeraWulf (WULF) – Application Software: 21.93%

Related ETFs: Investors seeking exposure to these industries may consider the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK), Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT), iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), First Trust Cloud Computing ETF (SKYY), and Global X Blockchain ETF (BKCH) for broader exposure to technology, software and digital infrastructure.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks receives fast-track inclusion on Israel's key indices: report
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) has received fast-track inclusion to appear on Israel's TA-35 and TA-125 indices, according to the Calcalist.

The fast track allows the cybersecurity company to be added on August 6, and its maximum weight on the indices will be capped at 5%, the report said. 

Palo Alto Networks began trading on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange in February 2026, after completing its acquisition of the Israel-based CyberArk, an identity security firm. Palo Alto was founded by Nir Zuk and Rajiv Batra in 2005.

data.title: Lucid reports Q2 deliveries, taps auto exec for top finance position
data.description:
Lucid manufactured 4,774 vehicles in the second quarter and delivered 3,953, an increase of 23% and 19%, respectively, from a year ago, but less than Wall Street expected.

Along with second quarter metrics, the company also announced organizational changes and streamlined its corporate suite, with the number of direct reports to CEO Silvio Napoli cut in half.

As part of these changes, auto executive Alexander De Bock has been named Lucid’s (LCID) new chief financial officer, replacing Taoufiq Boussaid, with Raja Ramana Macha tapped as chief technology officer.

Other appointments include Billy Hayes as chief customer officer, Hugo Martinho as chief transformation officer, Kay Stepper as president of Lucid Technologies and chief digital officer, and Christian Appel promoted to VP of Program Management.

These changes were announced last week and enable the company to reduce organizational complexity, align manufacturing capacity with market demand, and improve competitiveness.

Lucid (LCID) shares are down more than 3% on Thursday.
